title: Hope Beyond Anger
reading:
The New Testament gives us three more clear pictures of where unchecked anger leads.

King Herod, when he realized the wise men had not returned to him, was exceedingly angry — and had every male child in Bethlehem two years old and under put to death. A city of families destroyed because one man could not control his rage.

The chief priests, scribes, and elders assembled at the palace of Caiaphas and plotted to take Jesus by trickery and kill Him. Their anger at Jesus — the one who challenged their authority and exposed their hearts — drove them to conspire against the Son of God.

Judas Iscariot, indignant over what he saw as waste at Bethany, went from that moment directly to the chief priests and offered to betray Jesus for thirty pieces of silver. What started as anger and offense became the greatest act of betrayal in history.

The picture is consistent across every story in Scripture. People have died. Loved ones have been hurt. Some, because of one decision to be ruled by anger, are locked away in prison.

But here is where we land: we have hope in Jesus to break us free from the destruction of fleshy anger. Nothing good comes from it — Scripture is clear. Colossians 3:8 tells us to put it off. Not manage it. Put it off.

The Holy Spirit produces what anger destroys: love, peace, patience, gentleness, self-control. Seek Him daily — not only in ridding yourself of anger, but in becoming more like Him in all that you do.
reflection: What does it look like practically for you to put off anger and put on the character of Christ? What is one step you can take this week?
abide: Lord, I surrender what anger has built in me. Fill me with Your Spirit — not just to be less angry, but to be more like You. That's the only thing worth pursuing.
